title: Keep periodic sync but move correction authority onto a player-owned timeline
summary: 0.6.25 keeps the restored 0.6.24 core-player baseline intact and keeps the once-per-second sync cadence, but stops using AudioSource.time as the correction target. Instead, the periodic sync seam now follows a player-owned master timeline and uses clockResyncOccurred to keep that timeline anchored.
changes: Add a player-owned master timeline anchor to the standalone player record and keep the once-per-second sync cadence pointed at that internal timeline instead of AudioSource.time.
Wire VideoPlayer.clockResyncOccurred into the standalone runtime so Unity clock resync events refresh the player-owned timeline anchor instead of sitting as a dead hook.
Keep skipOnDrop = false in this slice and leave the surrounding playback, seek, focus, playlist, and surface behavior unchanged.
Stamp version 0.6.25 into player.version.json and BuildRuntimeInfo.cs.
Build the 0.6.25 raw core-player alpha seam with package inventory generated via -SkipVarDistribute.
reasoning: The restored 0.6.24 baseline brought back the last clearly working core-player behavior, but it also restored the 0.6.10 once-per-second AV correction seam.
The operator intent for that seam was always to keep periodic sync, not remove it. The actual bug is the authority model: the current code reads AudioSource.time and hard-seeks video to it.
This slice is intentionally narrow and avoids layering a new frame-drop posture, surface change, or playlist policy change on top.
operatorVisibleOutcomes: Movies should keep the once-per-second sync behavior, but correction should stop treating audio as hidden time authority.
This slice should feel like the 0.6.24 rollback baseline, but with the periodic sync seam following the player timeline instead of letting audio drag video around under stress.
knownIssues: This slice changes correction authority, not the 1-second cadence itself.

If stressed movie playback still degrades after this slice, the next lane should stay narrow and focus on transport timing or a different clock posture instead of dropping back to audio-driven hard seeks.
